speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 5. My name is Joe Bo brow. The survivor is Marion Gottesman. Today's date is June 26, 1996. Is there anything else you'd like to tell me about the life in the ghetto in Theresienstadt before we continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's put it this way that the young people tried to still educate themselves, have education from people who had educations already who taught us. And there were a lot of Jewish musicians. There were opera singers, Jewish opera singers, and they still put performances together even though they were not supposed to. Did it in hidden places, [BACKROUND NOISE] and they tried to, to make even in the ghetto a cultural life if possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to some of those concerts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I did. I had a friend who was playing at the [INAUDIBLE], played the [INAUDIBLE], and he was in the orchestra. And for rehearsals, I went with him in the basement somewhere, I remember. I know a girl who's, who's in Florida. She played Maria Stuart in and she was only 15 or 16 years old that time. And she had a fantastic voice and was a very good actress at the time already. She was from Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I don't know if they want me to mention names. I don't-- her name was Liana Peck. Actually, there were two sisters, Liana and the other one was-- I forgot her name. Both of them were playing in there, but Liana had the main lead. There also was a-- from I believe there is a Mortimer in there, and the boy was from Hamburg. And I believe he's a professor in California now with an [INAUDIBLE] already. And they tried to make still culture in, in the ghetto. And I feel that whatever they say about Theresienstadt, there was supposed to be, was supposed to be such a better ghetto than the other ones-- what made it better were the people in it, because they tried to keep dignity. And it helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about leaving Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did it happen, first of all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it happened around the 3rd of October, 1944. We got to Auschwitz in 19-- the 6th of October. So it must have been like three, four days before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no idea. Honestly, we had no idea. They had told us that there are labor camps around the Leipzig-Dresden area, which is not that far from-- you know, on the German side of the border instead of the Czechoslovakian. And we always heard about [GERMAN], which [GERMAN] happened to be a town in the-- is that [? Saxony ?] up there? I don't know. And they always said that people from the ghetto going to [GERMAN]. For what reason, who brought it up, I have no idea. So we thought we going either to another ghetto, or we have to work in [GERMAN] or whatever. Landed up in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with my mother and father together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go there? How were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I answer that, my mother and father were in the transport. I was not at that particular time, but I went anyway. I just took my things, and I went to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not supposed to go, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not-- I don't know if I wasn't supposed to go. I was not in that particular transport. I was not called in to go at that-- with that transport to Auschwitz. But being that my mother and father were in there, I wanted to go with them. I didn't know where I was going. And luckily I did, because I think that had something to do that my mother and I got out together. Saved our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you allowed to join the transport if your name was not--

speaker: interviewee
text: You were not allowed to do anything. You just did. Because it was all those people around there standing there with a little knapsack, not knowing what's happening. There were thousands of people in those transports. They didn't count you. They didn't ask your name. So I just went with them. I wasn't asked a name or anything. I just went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Went on what? What did you go on? How were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first we had to-- like a-- again, [NON-ENGLISH] like a place, collecting place in the ghetto. And from there they put us right there-- by then they had already the trains expanded from Auschwitz to Theresienstadt for the past year and a half before that. And right from that [NON-ENGLISH] you went onto the cattle trains with the SS standing there and the dogs already and et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle cars. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many per car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Full of people. Some could sit down. Some were standing up. No air. Nothing to eat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the minute we were in the, in those cars, we knew this was it. That was the last thing. I believed that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You thought the end was near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That-- I think-- and every one of us. And, you know, the train ride from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz-- it's something nobody can explain. You really can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to ask you to try anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't-- I mean, you can't explain it. You can't. It's impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take, first of all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think three days. Something like three days and nights. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were never allowed to get out of the car for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Couldn't get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for a break?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had no facilities. You had-- the cars were closed. No air coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't go to any bathroom. It was horrendous. OK? That was the trip. Were there guards in the car itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards, I believe, was on top of the cars, and then they had like those little wagons between the cattle cars where they were at.

speaker: interviewer
text: And each car had people from all ages? Old, young?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- no. Actually, were all my parents age and, and children and, and my age. At that time, I was 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't give you anything to eat or drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing to eat. Nothing to drink. No place to relieve yourself. I mean, it was like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dante's 'Inferno,' then already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did people relieve themselves? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just went. I mean, it, it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: --pail or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: --something when you think back over it, you can't really talk about it and explain, [BACKROUND NOISE] because it's the worst.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no pail--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to use? No bucket?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. Not that I know of. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you lie down?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Standing up for three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why couldn't you lie down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the wagons were so full of people that you couldn't. The few people who went in first maybe sat down, and everybody was tumbling on top of each other. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the people talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I really don't know. I really don't know. You heard a lot of crying. You heard people talking to each other, whispering to each other, holding each other. It was like something you cannot remember. You, you don't want to remember. I think that as bad as everything happened afterwards, I think that was the worst. That was the worst.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did some people die in the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen to them? What-- their bodies-- were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. The bodies were there, and when we got off the, the train, when they got us out of the train, they took the bodies out, and that was it. I have no idea, any idea what happened afterwards to other people than to ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the smell, the odor--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Incredible. It was terrible. Terrible. Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it something you could get used to after a while or-- and that it was not offensive--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if you could get--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the odor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course it's offensive. Of course you cannot get used to it, but you do not have a way out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try to get out of the car? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were banging on the doors. You know, they stopped the trains and [? said, ?] I guess, in the middle of the [INAUDIBLE] going up there on station. We couldn't even look out. Nobody opened up for us. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was no window, even? A little--

speaker: interviewee
text: No window.

speaker: interviewer
text: --small window?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Totally dark?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they're cattle cars. You know, where they have the-- over the cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the cattle cars finally stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stopped--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and you heard a lot of screaming from the SS, and a lot of [INAUDIBLE]. Then they opened up the thing, and [GERMAN], whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Means like Jews should get out. Of course, nobody else did anyway, right? Jews out. And we got out of there, and they put us in lines. And then there was an SS man standing there with a revolver in his hand. And he took my father on one side. You know, he pointed from one side to the other. Didn't talk. He pointed.

speaker: interviewer
text: With a revolver he was pointing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. With his hand and the revolver like that. So over to the left was to the left-- little did we know what the left or the right was-- and over to the right was the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father went to the left. My mother went to the left, and I went to the right. And my mother got out of the line in front-- at that time we didn't know who it was, but it was Mengele. And right in front of Mengele with the revolver in his hand started screaming at him. Not he at her-- Your mother screamed at him?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother screaming at him. You just took my husband away, who was fighting for you, for your fatherland. I remember exactly the words. I mean, I have to translate it in my head-- for your fatherland in 1914-18

speaker: interviewer
text: My daughter, you don't take away from me. Either she goes with me, or I go with her. We didn't know what is what, right? So he looked at my mother, and he said, how old are you? And my mother said something like 45. I think she was 46, 47, something. So-- how old is your daughter? So she made me automatically older, right? She said something like 19, 20. So he said, all right. Go on that side. So my mother got on the side where I was. And that's how we would get through that we got into-- not knowing we would get through. We didn't know what was happening to us. But you were separated from your father? Your father stayed--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were separated from my father, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother went to-- on your side.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother went, then, on my side, and we were-- were walked through a camp into some barracks, which turned out to be a sauna. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Turned out to be what?

speaker: interviewee
text: A sauna. You know, like what they call a sauna, like a cleansing place, whatever. And that's where they shaved our heads and our body hair and took everything away from us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back to your mother speaking out against this guard, who later turned out to be Mengele.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people surprised that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: --someone like-- would--

speaker: interviewee
text: He must have been very surprised--

speaker: interviewer
text: --would talk up, against--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that's for sure. He must have been very surprised, but people were in such a daze what was going on. Because we really didn't know what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you scared for your mother? Did you think something--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't scared for my mother. I was very scared for my father. Because I didn't know when my mother went on the left side what would happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then when they put my mother with me together, I said, all right. At least we are together, but what's going to happen to Papa? You know, and like that. That was it. He was 50 years old at the time. 50?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He had his 50th birthday still in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very courageous of your mother to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mother-- I think I mentioned it to you before-- her very, very courageous woman. Very. And this is not the last we saw of Mengele. We had an experience with him right before the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got-- well, we got into Auschwitz, as I told you. And then we-- later on, we were selected to a labor camp. And we landed up at [NON-ENGLISH], subsidiary of Gross-Rosen. And-- which it was called Marzdorf. And in March '45, Mengele must have gotten from-- Auschwitz was liberated in January, so he must have gotten before there to Gross-Rosen, not knowing to us. We didn't know. And we had a, a morning appell. You know, like take the count, et cetera. And what happened is here comes this SS man, which we didn't see before there with whole staff of people. And my mother and I were like in the first row in the center.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he stopped in front of my mother, and my mother had-- I mean, she looked bad already when we went to Auschwitz, but forget it. After that, I mean, that was-- a little skinny, tiny little woman, you know? No hair, et cetera. And he stopped in front of my mother, and he looked at her.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you only have to thank me that you are still alive, that you and your daughter are still alive. So he recognized my mother and me after all that. So which I, in my mind, believed that there were not many people who did that. Must have left an impression somehow or the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: He probably remembered that forever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went through some type of cleansing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --showers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were what they called-- actually, it was another selection, because there were some people who were taken away right at the time. You know, we had to get completely undressed, get our hair shaven, et cetera. Head shaven and body hair, et cetera. And then there were some-- which I don't know if there were other SS doctors or whatever they were, but they were officers. And they looked us up and down and took some of the people on the side. And my mother was on, on that side again, on the opposite side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Separated from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Separated from me. And I got out from my line and told my mother-- here we are, completely naked, no hair, no nothing. I said, Mama, they won't recognize you anyway. Go in the back of the line again. And whatever happened, she got through that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: She got through?

speaker: interviewee
text: She got-- you know, she got through on the line where I was. The others were taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she snuck back to your side--

speaker: interviewee
text: So she snuck back into the line where I was, right? But not behind-- directly behind me, on the end of the line. And she got through. So most likely, there was another SS doctor or another SS man or woman, whatever, and saw her and didn't recognize that she went through that line already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the line that she was on that she left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: --that they went right into the gas, because there were people who had scars on their body. And my mother had gone through when she was young a lot of surgeries. And I guess that's what the, the reason that they put her on that side.

speaker: interviewer
text: The younger people were on the line that you were on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the older, sicker people--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not old. The people weren't old in that particular-- people we were with. They were like maybe my mother's age. Let's say between 45 and 50, which maybe sounded old for me then. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were about how old now this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 17. I was 17 at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other teenagers? 17?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. A lot of them. And a few of us got through and, and made it alive and still meet as good friends here in the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the men separated from the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: The men were separated from the women, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: When they, they shaved you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did they use an actual razor blade shaver?

speaker: interviewee
text: Razor-- you know, like the razors, like the old German type of razors they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were shaven, like, completely--

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: --like to skin?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the skin.

speaker: interviewer
text: No hair at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: All parts of your body?

speaker: interviewee
text: All parts of your body.

speaker: interviewer
text: In front of the Germans, troops?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course. Men, everybody was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the-- it was such a shock. That you get like-- you're totally helpless. You don't-- and still you didn't know what was ahead of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any feeling left of, of trying to be modest and feel embarrassed to be naked in front of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any feelings like that, or was there hopelessness?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the only thing I remember is some women said it's a good thing that their husbands don't see them that way. That I remember how, how people acknowledge the fact what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the women screaming at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It was, like, mute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quiet. Mute. Nobody said a word. You were like-- I can't explain. It was almost like hypnosis, you know? You had nothing left in you to, to live for. And then they, then they gave us-- in another room, they gave us some clothes to wear. Not our clothes, some clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I landed up with those-- you know, like they look like the Dutch wooden shoes? The wooden shoes and a skirt and a jacket. No, a dress and a jacket.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the dress was all torn up and dirty, you know, and was open in front. No underwear, OK? And a jacket which was unbelievably nice. [INAUDIBLE] I had it for one day.

speaker: interviewee
text: A warm jacket on top of-- you know, it was October. Could have used a, a warm jacket. So here I was standing in the wooden shoes, in that dress. I don't know how long it was. I just know it was torn up to here. You could see everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I bit into the material to make a hole and ripped a piece out and bit holes in here to tie it together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK? Then I had that nice jacket. We get to the, to the barracks where we, you know, were put in, and they had the kapos, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't even think it took a day. The kapo took my jacket. So here I was without a jacket and a dress with holes. [LAUGHS] And that's how I got out of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother wearing? What kind of clothes did she have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same type-- we all had the same type of-- like we would say in Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH]. And torn and dirty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means what? Rags?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rags.

speaker: interviewer
text: They weren't official uniforms--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not uniforms--

speaker: interviewer
text: --striped?

speaker: interviewee
text: From other-- most likely from other prisoners. The clothes, which was very old already or used by some and taken off when they were gassed. Who knows?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a hat to cover your head at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, nothing. Nothing. Nothing at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we looked?

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking at the other people, thinking--

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, I felt we all look like alike.

speaker: interviewer
text: Realizing how you looked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We all looked alike. I mean, I was-- when I got to, to Auschwitz into the sauna there-- what I called a sauna-- there were people who I knew. Once the hair was off, you didn't know people anymore. You didn't even-- maybe recognize them by their eyes, but that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they shave the eyebrows, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not that I remember. No. No. Just the head and every other hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Underneath the arms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, underneath the arms and the rest of the body hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pubic area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the women molested by the German soldiers? Was there any type of sexual abuse?

speaker: interviewee
text: If there was, I, I didn't notice. I'll be honest. I don't know. I really don't know. I'm sure that was going on, but I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see it? You didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At that particular--

speaker: interviewer
text: --hear about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: --time, all you thought of, what's next?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the barracks look like that you were taken to now? Now you were dressed. You had beautiful clothes on.

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks were wooden barracks with a roof. And you went in, and there was like in the middle of the barrack-- I believe they must have been horse barracks at one time, you know, like for horses and animals?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the middle was like a walkway, a higher walkway. That's where the SS come back and forth on. On each side of them were what we called [? coverlets, ?] which-- the bunk beds, right? There were five on top of each other. Five levels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five levels. No straw or, or covers or anything. Just the wood and that's where [INAUDIBLE]-- on five at-- five people at a time on each.

speaker: interviewer
text: No mattress?

speaker: interviewee
text: No mattress. No covers. No nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pillow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Blanket?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. Just us.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was cold, wasn't it? This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was bitter cold. It was very cold, and it was in Poland. It's very cold there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep warm?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I have no idea. I know being very cold and very hungry [BACKROUND NOISE] and very dirty [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and feeling my head. Where's my hair? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother stay next to you on the bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and I, we were together all the time. We huddled together. Maybe that kept us warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you pass the time in the, in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being afraid, because by then we knew when we saw the smoke from the chimneys that they were extermination gas ovens. Being afraid from one minute to the other, now they take us. Because it was like a period of [INAUDIBLE] waiting and doing nothing. Once they either took us to what they called the latrine. You know, to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bathroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: --bathrooms. And that's-- one day, a girl approached me-- if I have a piece of bread to give her, she would give me a knife. And I still have the knife today. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You gave her a piece of your bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave her my bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she gave me-- I wanted a knife. Don't ask me why. But I had a knife.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was the big shot. I had a knife. Nothing to cut with, right? And-- But if you were so hungry, why did you give up the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I thought maybe with a knife I could save my life. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother know that you did this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course. She was right next to me. Everything happened together to us. From that minute on, everything happened together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was-- how did the day pass? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the morning was appell, which took for hours, standing in the cold, freezing till the SS can count it and count it and count it again. Just let us stand out there. And the rest of the time you were in the barrack, wondering what the next hour brings. You were looking ahead to get our soup once a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all you were fed? Once a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: All, all, all that-- what we got. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How much soup? How much--

speaker: interviewee
text: You used to have-- me, personally, I never talked about it. I didn't even write about it when I wrote it. We had a terrible experience. We had a soup next to us in big bucket. I don't even know how we ate it. And really now, I don't remember all that. I just know one thing. It must have been the third or fourth day in Auschwitz. There was a finger in there with a, with a ring. I have never, ever talked about it to anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a human finger?

speaker: interviewee
text: A human finger with a ring in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you ate the soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess we ate the soup. I don't know. But I remember that that was-- we were-- the five girls from [INAUDIBLE] that we sleeping on the bunk together, we were like in, in shock for days. I mean, you know, this is things which you really bury so deep down you don't even want to talk about it, but it happened. That happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the kapo in, in your barracks? Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't know any names from anybody. That didn't existed if you knew names. We didn't know any names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the kapo Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a Ukrainian kapo, which was very bad. And we had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a Jewish kapo, I believe there. We didn't really know what they-- I know one was Ukrainian, because she had a different-- they were in uniform, right? They had the striped suits and the hats and everything on. And the Ukrainian had a, a red triangle on, so we knew that she was a political or whatever. Turned out she was Ukrainian. But she was bad, really bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do that was bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Screaming and hitting and pushing and, and-- I think she's the one who took my jacket anyway, so that was bad enough for me. And the other one was a Jewish kapo, but I don't know if any of these people even survived, because they were there much longer than us and stayed much longer than us. And I'm sure they were gassed anyway or went on a death march.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you personally beaten at all in, in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. It was so bad that I really, really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother? Was she treated-- how was she treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is another thing. Coming back from the appell into the barrack, she got out of line, and she run to the electric wire, and she wanted to, wanted to kill herself. And just before she got to it, I had pulled her back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was Auschwitz. And two days later, they came and selected us for the labor camp. Did you know your mother was going to try to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --run to the fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just saw her running towards that, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about it afterwards to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We never spoke about it, ever. Not even here. We never talked about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to take another break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll resume in just a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a long story. [LAUGHS]
